title: Dog Spinal Sensitivity: What to Do When Your Dog's Back is Sore
meta_desc: Is your dog's spine sensitive to the touch? Learn about causes like IVDD, triage urgency levels, and how to help your vet diagnose the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Spinal sensitivity indicates pain along the backbone and requires veterinary attention within 24 hours, or immediately if your dog has trouble walking or controlling their bladder. Capturing a video of your dog's movement at home is a vital tool for helping your veterinarian reach an accurate diagnosis.

What is spinal sensitivity and what causes it in dogs?

Spinal sensitivity in dogs, often referred to as spinal hyperesthesia, is a condition where a dog exhibits signs of pain, flinching, or vocalization when the area along the backbone or neck is touched. This sensitivity can stem from various underlying issues, ranging from simple muscle strains to more serious conditions like Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), arthritis, or inflammatory disorders of the nervous system. Because the spine houses the central nervous system, any sign of discomfort in this area warrants careful observation.

Is my dog's spinal sensitivity a veterinary emergency?

  • Medium Urgency: General spinal sensitivity should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 hours to address pain and identify the cause.
  • High Urgency: Seek immediate veterinary intervention if the sensitivity is accompanied by 'knuckling' (walking on the tops of the paws), an inability to stand, or loss of bladder control.
  • Risk of Paralysis: Severe neurological symptoms require emergency care to prevent the possibility of permanent paralysis.

How can a video of my dog help the veterinarian with triage?

  • Identifying Masked Pain: Dogs often experience an adrenaline surge at the clinic that masks their pain, making it difficult for the vet to see the true extent of the discomfort.
  • Home Environment Observation: A video taken in a calm setting allows the clinical team to see the exact 'hunch' in the back or the specific gait abnormality as it happens naturally.
  • Faster Diagnosis: Providing visual evidence of the symptoms helps the veterinary team reach a more accurate diagnosis and treatment plan quickly.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

For dogs experiencing spinal pain with minimal to moderate neurologic deficits, 2-3 weeks of strict cage rest is often recommended. A short course of prednisone (0.5 mg/kg/day for 3 days) may help alleviate pain. However, anti-inflammatory or analgesic medications should not be used without concurrent cage rest, as increased activity may worsen spinal cord compression. In cases of severe neurologic deficits, failure of conservative therapy, or recurrent episodes, prompt surgery offers the best chance of recovery, typically involving hemilaminectomy with removal of extruded disc material. Definitive diagnosis relies on imaging studies, though radiographs are not sensitive or specific enough, often requiring myelography, MRI, or CT.

Chapter: Neurology, Surgery

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1247)

faqs.question: What are the common signs of back pain in dogs?
faqs.content: Common signs include a hunched posture, shivering or trembling, reluctance to jump or climb stairs, and crying out when picked up.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog over-the-counter pain relief?
faqs.content: No. Never give human medications like Ibuprofen, Aspirin, or Tylenol to a dog without veterinary supervision, as these can be highly toxic or cause severe stomach ulcers.

faqs.question: How should I transport a dog with a sensitive spine?
faqs.content: Keep the dog as still as possible. Use a crate or a flat board for support, and try to minimize any twisting or bending of the back during the move to the car.
